name: Financial Terminology
description: Equities, accounting, valuation, and fixed income terms drawn from intro-level finance and CFA Level 1.

cards.definition: Price-to-Earnings: market price per share divided by earnings per share. A multiple of how much investors pay per dollar of earnings.
Earnings Per Share: net income (less preferred dividends) divided by weighted-average shares outstanding.
Share price × shares outstanding. The equity-only value of a public company.
Sensitivity of a stock's returns to market returns. β=1 moves with the market; β>1 is more volatile; β<0 is inversely correlated.
Annual dividends per share divided by share price. A trailing measure of cash return.
Shareholders' equity per the balance sheet — assets minus liabilities, on accounting rather than market terms.
Shares actually available for public trading — outstanding shares minus restricted/insider holdings.
Number of shares sold short but not yet covered. Often expressed as a percentage of float.
Excess return relative to a benchmark after adjusting for risk (beta). Positive α means outperformance.
Return on Equity: net income divided by average shareholders' equity. Profitability per unit of book equity.
Return on Invested Capital: NOPAT divided by invested capital (debt + equity − cash). Capital-structure-agnostic profitability.
Market cap + total debt + minority interest + preferred − cash and equivalents. The price to acquire the whole business, debt-included.
Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. Proxy for operating cash generation.
Operating cash flow minus capex. Cash available to investors after maintaining the asset base.
Reduction in existing shareholders' ownership when new shares are issued (options, secondary offerings, M&A).
Recognising revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash timing.
Expenses are recorded in the same period as the revenues they helped generate.
Assumption that the entity will continue operating into the foreseeable future, justifying long-lived asset values.
Threshold above which omitting or misstating an item would influence a reasonable user's decision.
When uncertainty exists, prefer estimates that understate assets/income rather than overstate them.
ASC 606 / IFRS 15 5-step process: identify contract, performance obligations, transaction price, allocate, recognise as obligations are satisfied.
Allocating the cost of a tangible long-lived asset over its useful life as expense.
Allocating the cost of an intangible asset (or a debt premium/discount) over time as expense.
Inventory cost-flow assumptions. FIFO sells oldest cost first (higher income in inflation); LIFO sells newest first (lower income/taxes in inflation; banned under IFRS).
Excess of purchase price over fair value of net identifiable assets in an acquisition. Tested for impairment, not amortised under US GAAP.
A liability — cash received before the goods/services have been delivered.
Current assets minus current liabilities. Short-term operational liquidity buffer.
Discounted Cash Flow: present value of projected free cash flows plus a terminal value, discounted at WACC.
Weighted-Average Cost of Capital: blended after-tax cost of debt and equity, weighted by market values. The DCF discount rate.
Value of cash flows beyond the explicit forecast horizon — typically Gordon Growth or exit-multiple method. Often the bulk of a DCF's value.
Terminal value = CF / (r − g). Assumes constant perpetual growth; sensitive to g approaching r.
Valuation by applying peer trading multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/Sales) to the target's metrics.
Valuation using multiples paid in past M&A deals for similar targets — typically includes a control premium.
Leveraged Buyout: acquisition financed primarily with debt against the target's cash flows. Returns driven by debt paydown, multiple expansion, EBITDA growth.
Net Asset Value: assets minus liabilities, marked to fair value. Standard valuation for funds and asset-heavy businesses.
Share price divided by book value per share. Often used for banks and other balance-sheet-driven businesses.
Enterprise value divided by EBITDA. Capital-structure-neutral multiple comparable across leverage profiles.
P/E divided by expected earnings growth rate. Adjusts P/E for growth — PEG ≈ 1 is rule-of-thumb 'fairly priced.'
Discount of market price below intrinsic value. Buffer against valuation error or adverse outcomes (Graham/Buffett).
An estimate of true economic value based on fundamentals — typically the present value of future free cash flows.
Annual interest paid by a bond as a percentage of its face/par value. Fixed for plain-vanilla bonds.
Internal rate of return on a bond held to maturity, given current price and remaining cash flows.
Weighted-average time to receive bond cash flows; also a sensitivity measure: %ΔPrice ≈ −D × ΔYield.
Second-order sensitivity of bond price to yield changes — captures the curvature duration misses for large moves.
Yield difference between a credit-risky bond and a comparable-maturity Treasury. Compensation for default risk.
Face value of a bond — the amount repaid at maturity. Coupon rate is applied to this.
Bond issued at a discount to par and paying no coupons; return is the accretion to face value at maturity.
Bond the issuer can redeem before maturity, typically when rates fall. Investors demand higher yield for the call risk.
Short-term Treasury yields exceed long-term yields. Historically a leading indicator of recession.
Issuer/issue creditworthiness ranking from agencies (S&P/Moody's/Fitch). Investment grade ≥ BBB-/Baa3; below is high-yield.
